[Term]
id: GO:0000017
name: alpha-glucoside transport
namespace: biological_process
def: "The directed movement of alpha-glucosides into, out of or within a cell, or between cells, by means of some agent such as a transporter or pore. Alpha-glucosides are glycosides in which the sugar group is a glucose residue, and the anomeric carbon of the bond is in an alpha configuration." [GOC:jl, ISBN:0198506732, PMID:9919658]
intersection_of: GO:0006810 ! transport
intersection_of: has_primary_input CHEBI:60979 ! alpha-glucoside

[Term]
id: GO:0000041
name: transition metal ion transport
namespace: biological_process
def: "The directed movement of transition metal ions into, out of or within a cell, or between cells, by means of some agent such as a transporter or pore. A transition metal is an element whose atom has an incomplete d-subshell of extranuclear electrons, or which gives rise to a cation or cations with an incomplete d-subshell. Transition metals often have more than one valency state. Biologically relevant transition metals include vanadium, manganese, iron, copper, cobalt, nickel, molybdenum and silver." [ISBN:0198506732]
synonym: "transition metal transport" EXACT []
intersection_of: GO:0006810 ! transport
intersection_of: has_primary_input CHEBI:33515 ! transition element cation

{"body":"**GO:0005615 - extracellular space** is often confused with the more general parent extracellular region. There is a taxon constraint but I think it would be more intuitive to use its related synonym **intercellular space** which is, in my opinion, a lot less confusing since it would contain the "multicellular" information.
Cheers,
Marc.","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7n7nPA","author":{"login":"pgaudet"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"@marcfeuermann and I are looking at the top-level of the CC hierarchy. 

We propose to obsolete GO:0005615 extracellular space and replace by GO:0005576 extracellular region.   

CURRENT DEFINITIONS:
**GO:0005615 - extracellular space: definition:** 
That part of a multicellular organism outside the cells proper, usually taken to be outside the plasma membranes, and occupied by fluid.
_Comment: Note that for multicellular organisms, the extracellular space refers to everything outside a cell, but still within the organism (excluding the extracellular matrix). Gene products from a multi-cellular organism that are secreted from a cell into the interstitial fluid or blood can therefore be annotated to this term._
Current children include some secreted protein complexes, as well as 
GO:0034358    plasma lipoprotein particle and
GO:0070062    extracellular exosome
[GO:0034358](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0034358)    plasma lipoprotein particle	part_of
[GO:0070062](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070062)    extracellular exosome

**GO:0005576 extracellular region: definition:** 
The space external to the outermost structure of a cell. For cells without external protective or external encapsulating structures this refers to space outside of the plasma membrane. This term covers the host cell environment outside an intracellular parasite.
_Comment: Note that this term is intended to annotate gene products that are not attached to the cell surface. For gene products from multicellular organisms which are secreted from a cell but retained within the organism (i.e. released into the interstitial fluid or blood), consider the cellular component term 'extracellular space ; GO:0005615'._

The reason for the obsoletion/merge is that these terms have been confused and the distinction is quite subtle and not very informative. For proteins secreted in multicellular organisms, for example in blood and other body fluids, the informative aspect of localization cannot be captured with GO; but only with [UBERON](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0006314).  

We can discuss this at the next GO Monthly Update call if needed.

Thanks, Pascale

## Ontology-internal impact

  15 CC terms with part_of GO:0005615: 12 already also have part_of GO:0005576 — just delete the redundant line. The remaining 3 (inhibin complex,
  extracellular exosome, reelin complex) need a simple swap to part_of GO:0005576.

  2 BP logical definitions:
  - intercellular transport (GO:0010496): uses results_in_transport_across GO:0005615. This is the one case requiring curator judgment — "transport across
  extracellular region" is semantically weaker than "across the intercellular space." May need a revised logical def or a different filler term.
  - ferric triacetylfusarinine C import (GO:0015686): uses has_target_start_location GO:0005615. Replacing with extracellular region is a safe generalization.

  1 taxon constraint (never_in Ascomycota): becomes moot upon obsoletion.

  3 slim memberships (goslim_generic, goslim_drosophila, goslim_chembl): need remapping to GO:0005576 (which is already in most slims).

cat << 'EOF' > /tmp/issue_response.md
## Diagnosis

The misclassification is caused by the equivalent-class (logical) definition of `GO:0010496` interacting with property chains that propagate through `part_of`.

### Current equivalent-class axiom on `intercellular transport`

```
[Term]
id: GO:0010496
name: intercellular transport
intersection_of: GO:0006810                                ! transport
intersection_of: has_target_start_location CL:0000000      ! cell
intersection_of: has_target_end_location   CL:0000000      ! cell
```

i.e. **`intercellular transport ≡ transport ⊓ (has_target_start_location some cell) ⊓ (has_target_end_location some cell)`**

This is the new logical definition introduced as part of issue #31545 (obsoletion of `extracellular space`, which the prior `results_in_transport_across GO:0005615` axiom pointed at). The new axiom is what ValWood is seeing in the change log on 2026‑03‑06.

### Why the children become intracellular transports

In RO, both location relations have a `part_of` propagation chain:

```
has_target_start_location o part_of  SubPropertyOf  has_target_start_location
has_target_end_location   o part_of  SubPropertyOf  has_target_end_location
```

And in GO/CL, intracellular organelles are explicitly `part_of some cell`:

```
intracellular anatomical structure (GO:0005622)  SubClassOf  part_of some cell (CL:0000000)
endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi, nuclear lumen, cytosol, …    SubClassOf  intracellular membrane‑bounded organelle  SubClassOf  intracellular anatomical structure
```

So **any organelle is, in OWL, `part_of some cell`**, and the property chain therefore lets the reasoner conclude that a transport whose start/end is an organelle is also a transport whose start/end is *a* cell. The equivalence axiom then snaps it into `intercellular transport`.

### `robot explain` for one example — ER → Golgi vesicle-mediated transport (GO:0006888)

```
endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport
   EquivalentTo
     vesicle-mediated transport
     and occurs_in some cytoplasm
     and has_target_start_location some endoplasmic reticulum
     and has_target_end_location   some Golgi apparatus

endoplasmic reticulum       SubClassOf  intracellular membrane-bounded organelle
Golgi apparatus             SubClassOf  intracellular membrane-bounded organelle
intracellular membrane-bounded organelle
                            EquivalentTo  membrane-bounded organelle
                                          and part_of some intracellular anatomical structure
intracellular anatomical structure
                            SubClassOf  part_of some cell

has_target_start_location o part_of  SubPropertyOf  has_target_start_location
has_target_end_location   o part_of  SubPropertyOf  has_target_end_location

intercellular transport
   EquivalentTo
     transport
     and has_target_start_location some cell
     and has_target_end_location   some cell
```

Following the chain: ER → intracellular anatomical structure → `part_of some cell`. Therefore the start ER **becomes** "a cell" under the property chain, and likewise for Golgi at the end. Both legs of the equivalence are satisfied, so ER→Golgi vesicle-mediated transport is entailed to be `intercellular transport`. The same proof template applies to nuclear export, late endosome→vacuole transport, etc. — essentially anything in `intracellular transport` whose start/end are organelles.

### Biologist-friendly version

The defining axiom says, in plain English: *"intercellular transport is any transport that starts in a cell and ends in a cell."* That sounds right at first reading, but in OWL **"in a cell"** is interpreted via the part-of hierarchy. Because the nucleus is part of a cell, the cytosol is part of a cell, the ER is part of a cell, etc., **any intracellular transport already satisfies "starts in a cell and ends in a cell"**. The reasoner has no way to demand that the *start cell* and the *end cell* be **two different cells** — that is exactly the constraint we mean by "between cells", and standard description-logic profile (EL/ELK) cannot express it.

So nuclear export trivially qualifies: it starts in the nuclear lumen (which is part of a cell) and ends in the cytosol (which is part of the same cell). The intent ("from one cell to another") is captured in the text definition but not in the formal logic.

The only child that escapes this trap is `vesicle-mediated intercellular transport` (GO:0110077): its differentia is `process_has_causal_agent extracellular vesicle`, which doesn't get pulled in by intracellular-transport children. The plasmodesmata- and gap-junction-mediated children are also safe for the same reason.

### Why this only appeared recently

Before issue #31545, the equivalent-class axiom for `intercellular transport` used `results_in_transport_across GO:0005615` (extracellular space). That filler kept the definition tight — intracellular transports do not cross the extracellular space. When `GO:0005615` was obsoleted, the logical definition was rewritten using `has_target_start_location` / `has_target_end_location` on `CL:0000000`, which turns out to be far too loose, for the reason above.

### Suggested fixes (for curator judgment)

1. **Demote to non-defining axioms.** Drop the `intersection_of` lines and re-assert the start/end as plain `relationship: has_target_start_location CL:0000000` / `has_target_end_location CL:0000000` (or drop them entirely). The three real children (`GO:0010497`, `GO:0110077`, `GO:1990349`) already assert `intersection_of: GO:0010496` directly, so they stay correctly placed; intracellular transports stop being pulled in.
2. **Use `results_in_transport_across` with a cell-boundary filler** (e.g. plasma membrane `GO:0005886`) — analogous to the previous use of extracellular space, but staying within current CC. This would actually distinguish "crosses a cell boundary" from "moves between two organelles of the same cell." The catch: choosing a filler that doesn't itself have part-of links back into the cell interior.
3. **Restore `results_in_transport_across some extracellular region (GO:0005576)`**, accepting the "weaker than extracellular space" caveat that @cmungall already flagged in #31545. Extracellular region is *not* `part_of some cell`, so the property chain does not pull intracellular transports in.

Option 1 is the safest immediate fix; option 3 most closely matches the pre-#31545 semantics. Happy to implement whichever is preferred.

---

- Agent harness: claude-code
- Triggered by: @cmungall

content.text:
Diagnosis posted to issue #32166.

**Root cause in one line:** the post-#31545 equivalent-class axiom on `GO:0010496` — `transport ⊓ has_target_start_location some cell ⊓ has_target_end_location some cell` — is satisfied by every intracellular transport, because RO's `has_target_*_location o part_of ⊑ has_target_*_location` chains combined with `intracellular anatomical structure SubClassOf part_of some cell` make every organelle effectively "in a cell". The DL profile (EL) can't express "two different cells", so the formal definition does not match the textual intent ("between cells").

I included a `robot explain` proof tree for ER→Golgi vesicle-mediated transport (GO:0006888), a biologist-friendly explanation, the history (why this only appeared after `GO:0005615` was obsoleted in #31545), and three candidate fixes for curator judgment. No edits made — I was asked to diagnose, not patch.
